title: STL: Algorithms
description: The `<algorithm>` header is a toolkit of 100+ ready-made functions. A competitive programmer who masters sort, binary search, and the key numeric/permutation algorithms can solve problems in 10 lines that a beginner would spend 50 lines on. This chapter covers the algorithms that appear most frequently in Codeforces problems up to 1800 rating.

sections.content: `std::sort` uses **introsort** (hybrid quicksort + heapsort + insertion sort) and runs in **O(n log n)** worst case. It sorts **in-place** on random-access iterators (vector, array, deque). You can pass a custom comparator — a function or lambda — to control sort order.

sections.title: sort — All Forms

sections.label: Strict Weak Ordering — Invalid Comparators Cause UB
sections.content: A comparator `cmp(a, b)` MUST satisfy strict weak ordering: `cmp(x, x)` must be false (irreflexivity). A common bug is `return a.val <= b.val` — using `<=` instead of `<` violates this rule and causes undefined behavior (crash, corruption, infinite loop). Always use strict `<`.

The STL provides three binary search functions on **sorted** ranges. In CP, `lower_bound` and `upper_bound` are the workhorses — they return iterators, not booleans, giving you exact positions to work with.

sections.title: lower_bound / upper_bound — CP Patterns

sections.content: `accumulate`, `partial_sum`, `adjacent_difference`, and `iota` from `<numeric>` are powerful one-liners that replace common loop patterns. Know them — they save time and reduce bugs.

sections.title: Numeric Algorithms — CP Applications

sections.content: `next_permutation` and `prev_permutation` cycle through all orderings of a sequence. They return `false` when the sequence wraps around (from last permutation to first or vice versa). Used in brute-force enumeration problems when n ≤ 8.

sections.title: Permutations and Useful Rearrangements

sections.label: unique Does NOT Remove All Duplicates Alone
sections.content: `unique()` only removes **consecutive** duplicates and returns an iterator to the new logical end — it does not resize the vector. You must `erase()` the tail afterwards. And the vector must be **sorted first** for this to remove all duplicates. The `sort + unique + erase` trio is a classic one-liner CP idiom.

sections.title: find, count, min_element, max_element
Exercise 1: lower_bound and upper_bound Index Arithmetic
sections.explanation: `lower_bound(4)` → index 1 (first 4). `upper_bound(4)` → index 4 (first element > 4, which is 6). Distance = 3 occurrences of 4. `lower_bound(5)` returns iterator to first element >= 5, which is 6. `binary_search(3)` returns false (0) because 3 is not in the vector.

sections.title: Exercise 2: next_permutation Return Value
sections.explanation: Starting from {1,3,2}: next → {2,1,3} (returns 1). Next → {2,3,1} (returns 1). Next → {3,1,2} (returns 1). The sequence follows lexicographic permutation order. It would return 0 only when wrapping from {3,2,1} back to {1,2,3}.

sections.title: Exercise 3: accumulate with Initial Value Type Trap
sections.explanation: Line 1: Initial value `0` is `int`. The sum 600000 overflows 32-bit int (max ~2.1 billion — actually 600000 fits, but the accumulate result type is determined by the initial value type). Wait — 100000+200000+300000 = 600000 which fits in int. Actually the output for line 1 is 600000 too. But line 3: 100000*200000*300000 = 6*10^15 which absolutely overflows int, giving garbage. Let me recalculate: the answer is 600000, 600000, and an overflow value for line 3.

Lines 1 & 2: Sum is 600000, well within int range, so both print correctly. Line 3: Product 100000 × 200000 × 300000 = 6×10^15 which massively overflows `int` (max ≈ 2.1×10^9), producing an implementation-defined garbage value. To fix: use `0LL` and `long long` in the lambda accumulator.

sections.title: Exercise 4: Find the Bug — Custom Sort Crash
sections.error: The comparator `a >= b` violates strict weak ordering because `cmp(x, x) = (x >= x) = true` (irreflexivity is violated). Using `>=` instead of `>` is undefined behavior and may cause an infinite loop, crash, or incorrect output.
sections.explanation: The rule: a comparator `cmp(a, b)` must return `true` if and only if `a` comes strictly before `b`. When `a == b`, it MUST return `false`. Using `>=` returns `true` for equal elements, breaking the contract and causing undefined behavior in `std::sort`.

sections.title: Exercise 5: Codeforces-Style — Sort Strings by Custom Key
sections.approach: Write a lambda comparator that encodes the three-level sort key. Compute distinct char count via a `set<char>` or bitmask. Compare all three criteria with a clean `if/else if/else` chain, always using strict `<`.
sections.explanation: The three-level comparator cleanly falls through each criterion. The `distinct` lambda recomputes per comparison call — for a cleaner O(n log n) solution, precompute distinct counts into a vector and compare those. The `set<char>` trick is elegant and correct since the alphabet is only 26 characters.

items: **`sort(b,e)`** — O(n log n), in-place. Add comparator lambda for custom order. Use strict `<` only.
**`lower_bound(b,e,v)`** — first element **>= v**. Subtract `.begin()` for index. O(log n).
**`upper_bound(b,e,v)`** — first element **> v**. `upper - lower` = count of v. O(log n).
**`accumulate(b,e,init)`** — sum/fold. Use `0LL` as init when values may overflow int.
**`partial_sum(b,e,out)`** — writes prefix sums to output range. O(n).
**`next_permutation(b,e)`** — advances to next permutation, returns false after last. Requires sorted start.
**`unique(b,e)`** — removes consecutive duplicates, returns new end. Must sort first for full dedup.
**`count_if(b,e,pred)`** — count elements satisfying predicate. **`find_if`** returns iterator to first match.
**`iota(b,e,start)`** — fills range with start, start+1, start+2… Great for index arrays.
